fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need a native interop utility library in Dart (the ffi package). Basically devs keep complaining that every time they need to talk to native code they end up writing the same allocation/cleanup boilerplate over and over, and then someone forgets to free memory on an error path and we get a leak. We want a clean solution for two main pain points: (1) string handling — converting text back and forth between Dart strings and native memory buffers, both for the UTF-8 stuff and the wide-char stuff Windows likes. Make sure edge cases like funky characters and embedded nulls work properly, similar to how we handled encoding boundaries in that auth module a while back. (2) resource lifetime management — we need scoped cleanup that works for sync code, async code, and when things blow up with an exception. There's also a reusable variant that some internal tooling relies on where the release callback might itself allocate things, so that needs to be handled cleanly. Oh and errors should come back in a portable, runtime-neutral format rather than whatever Dart happens to throw. The test harness reads JSON from stdin and writes plain-text key=value lines to stdout, so the I/O adapter needs to stay decoupled from the actual logic. Check the existing test files to understand the exact expected output shapes.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: void main() async — stdin JSON dispatch adapter calling NativeTextConverter and NativeResourceScope domain objects

trigger_keywords: UTF-8 encoding output format
what does the native_units array contain
how is the zero terminator represented in UTF-8 output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For UTF-8 text_to_native_units the output must have three lines: encoding=utf8, native_units=[...] where the array contains every encoded byte INCLUDING the trailing zero byte (e.g. [72,101,...,10,0]), and terminator=0. The zero terminator is part of the native_units list, not a separate count.

trigger_keywords: UTF-8 decoding without length field
what happens when no length is provided for decoding
stop at null byte behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When decoding UTF-8 (native_units_to_text) without a 'length' field, decoding stops before the first zero byte — the zero byte is NOT included in text_code_units or the returned text. Output lines are: encoding=utf8, text_json="...", text_code_units=[...] where the array excludes the terminator byte.

trigger_keywords: UTF-8 decoding with explicit length
length field behavior for UTF-8
embedded null bytes with length
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a 'length' field is supplied for native_units_to_text, exactly that many bytes are decoded regardless of zero bytes within them — embedded zero bytes are preserved in the text. This allows round-tripping binary data that contains null bytes.

trigger_keywords: invalid UTF-8 error format
what error is returned for bad bytes
malformed byte sequence error output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For invalid UTF-8 byte sequences the output must be exactly two lines: error=invalid_text_encoding followed by encoding=utf8. The error category name is 'invalid_text_encoding', not a Dart exception class name.

trigger_keywords: negative length error format
what happens with negative length value
negative_length error output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a negative 'length' field is provided, the output must include error=negative_length and field=length (the name of the offending field). These are language-neutral portable error fields, not exception stack traces.

trigger_keywords: null pointer error format
decoding a null native pointer
null_pointer error output fields
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For null pointer decoding or measurement attempts the output must include error=null_pointer, operation=<the attempted operation e.g. native_units_to_text or measure_native_units>, and encoding=<utf8 or utf16>.

trigger_keywords: UTF-16 encoding output format
native_units array for UTF-16
surrogate pair representation in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For UTF-16 text_to_native_units the output lines are: encoding=utf16, native_units=[...] listing each UTF-16 code unit as a plain integer (not byte pairs) including the trailing zero code unit, and terminator=0. Surrogate pairs appear as two consecutive code unit integers before the terminator.

trigger_keywords: UTF-16 decoding direction field value
round_trip_text direction for UTF-16
what direction value triggers UTF-16 decode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: UTF-16 decoding uses direction=round_trip_text (not native_units_to_text). The input provides a 'text' field which is encoded to UTF-16 native units and then decoded back. Output lines: encoding=utf16, text_json="...", text_code_units=[...] excluding the zero terminator.

trigger_keywords: UTF-16 length measurement output
measure_native_units for UTF-16
unit_count field meaning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For UTF-16 measure_native_units the output is: encoding=utf16, unit_count=N where N is the number of UTF-16 code units before the first zero terminator, NOT counting the terminator itself.

trigger_keywords: explicit scope resource cleanup output format
released_during_scope vs released_after_scope
what lines does the explicit scope feature output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For explicit resource scopes the output is: scope=synchronous (or asynchronous), released_during_scope=[...] (always empty while body runs), released_after_scope=[...] listing resource IDs released after scope exits. For exceptional completion also include error=<error_message>.

trigger_keywords: asynchronous scope cleanup timing
when are resources released in async scope
async completion resource release
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In an asynchronous explicit scope, resources must remain unreleased (released_during_scope=[]) while the async body is running and only appear in released_after_scope after the Future/async result completes. The scope output line should read scope=asynchronous.

trigger_keywords: scoped allocation cleanup output
allocated_blocks and freed_blocks fields
what does feature9 output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For scoped allocation scenarios the output reports allocated_blocks=N and freed_blocks=N. Allocating 'two_i64_values' counts as 1 allocation block and must be freed exactly once when the scope exits, so both values equal 1. If raise_during_scope=true also include an error line before counts.

trigger_keywords: ambient scope output format
how does ambient scope differ from explicit scope output
scope= line value for ambient
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Ambient scope output uses scope=ambient_synchronous (not scope=synchronous) for sync completion, scope=ambient_asynchronous for async, and scope=ambient_error for exceptional completion. The released_during_scope and released_after_scope fields follow the same rules as explicit scopes.

trigger_keywords: ambient asynchronous error propagation output
handled_error and uncaught_error fields
what does feature11 output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For ambient asynchronous error propagation the output is three lines: async_result=<fallback_result value>, handled_error=yes (confirming the caller caught it), uncaught_error=no (confirming the guarded context saw no unhandled rejection). The fallback_result integer from the input appears verbatim.

trigger_keywords: reusable scope output format
release callback allocation tracking
allocated_after_release and freed_after_release meaning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a reusable scope where the release callback itself allocates, the output must have four lines: allocated_before_release=1, freed_before_release=0, allocated_after_release=2, freed_after_release=2. The callback allocation is tracked and freed before release() returns, so after_release counts include both the original and the callback allocation.

trigger_keywords: JSON I/O adapter decoupling
how does stdin JSON map to core logic
separation between I/O and domain
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects fields like 'encoding', 'direction', 'resource_scope', and 'completion' to route to the appropriate core domain method (NativeTextConverter or NativeResourceScope), then formats results as key=value lines to stdout. The core domain must have no dependency on dart:io, JSON parsing, or stdout.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled encoding boundaries in that auth module a while back
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The UTF-8 and UTF-16 error normalization logic described in Feature 4 and Feature 7 — specifically that malformed byte sequences map to error=invalid_text_encoding and boundary cases (negative length, null pointer) map to their own portable error category fields rather than Dart-native exception types.

context_pointers.vague_reference: a reusable variant that some internal tooling relies on where the release callback might itself allocate things
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 12 (reusable_scope): a NativeResourceScope variant that, when releasing resources, tracks and frees any allocations made by the release callback itself before release() returns, verified by the allocated_before_release / freed_before_release / allocated_after_release / freed_after_release output fields in feature12_reusable_scope_release.json.

UTF-16 encoding/decoding (Features 5–7): text_to_native_units with encoding=utf16 producing integer code units, round_trip_text direction for decoding, and measure_native_units for unit_count, all mirroring the UTF-8 surface but operating on 16-bit code units.

context_pointers.vague_reference: errors should come back in a portable, runtime-neutral format
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The error output schema defined in Feature 4 and Feature 7: use fields error=invalid_text_encoding, error=negative_length with field=<name>, or error=null_pointer with operation=<op> and encoding=<enc> — never Dart exception class names or stack traces.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect in the current requirement notes. Please check the existing test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the PRD in start.md for the exact output format contract — the test cases are the authoritative black-box spec for what each feature must produce.

feature10_ambient_scoped_cleanup.json.description: A ambient native-resource context releases resources after synchronous, asynchronous, and exceptional completion.

feature11_ambient_error_propagation.json.description: Errors from asynchronous work inside a ambient resource context remain catchable by the caller and are not reported as uncaught errors.

feature12_reusable_scope_release.json.description: When a reusable resource scope releases resources, allocations requested during release are also tracked and freed before release returns.

feature1_utf8_native_encoding.json.description: Converting text to zero-terminated UTF-8 native byte units preserves ASCII and multi-byte characters.

feature2_utf8_native_decoding.json.description: Decoding UTF-8 native byte units returns the requested text, using either the first zero terminator or an explicit byte length.

feature3_utf8_native_length.json.description: Measuring a zero-terminated UTF-8 native byte sequence reports the number of bytes before the first zero byte.

feature4_utf8_error_normalization.json.description: Invalid UTF-8 inputs and unsupported null or negative-length requests are reported with language-neutral error categories.

feature5_utf16_native_encoding.json.description: Converting text to zero-terminated UTF-16 native code units preserves ASCII, line breaks, and surrogate-pair characters.

feature6_utf16_native_decoding.json.description: Decoding UTF-16 native code units returns text up to the zero terminator unless an explicit unit length is supplied.

feature7_utf16_length_and_errors.json.description: UTF-16 native code unit measurement and unsupported requests expose counts or normalized error categories.

feature8_scoped_resource_cleanup.json.description: A scoped native-resource context releases registered resources after synchronous, asynchronous, and exceptional completion.

feature9_scoped_allocation_cleanup.json.description: Memory allocated within a scoped native-resource context is released once when the scope finishes, including exceptional completion and text conversion allocations.
